exemplar.title: To what extent has the Iraqi constitution, since 2006, negatively affected sectarian and ethnic divisions in the Iraqi population.

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent has the Iraqi constitution, since 2006, negatively affected sectarian and ethnic divisions in the Iraqi population.

strengths: Issue and scope are stated explicitly and unambiguously, demonstrating full clarity of the political issue.
Personal motivation is clearly explained, connecting heritage to topic choice.
Direct, clear links are made between the engagement, issue and course content units (sovereignty, legitimacy, peace and conflict).
weaknesses: Justification of choice of specific interviewees could be deepened by explaining why their perspectives were uniquely suited.
Course concepts are listed rather than embedded throughout the discussion, limiting depth of theoretical linkage.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets all topâband descriptors: the issue is explicit, personal motivation is clear, and course content is linked. Minor weaknesses in the depth of content linkage and interviewee selection justify the full mark of 4.

strengths: Clear and relevant description of both interviews, explaining what was done and why it matters for the issue.
Strong, concrete reflections showing how each engagement informed the studentâs understanding.
weaknesses: Lacks methodological detail (dates, medium, duration) that would make the description fully comprehensive.
Occasional stray formatting (LaTeX) disrupts narrative clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The engagement is described clearly and relevance is evident (band 3â4), but missing methodological specifics prevents a topâband mark of 4. Learning reflections are strong, justifying a solid mark of 3 overall.

strengths: Analysis applies key concepts (legitimacy, state failure, identity politics) effectively.
Causal links between constitutional design and sectarian outcomes are clear and largely justified.
Counter-arguments are acknowledged and weighed, demonstrating critical engagement.
weaknesses: A few assertions (e.g., Kurdish control of Baghdad, drafting time frame) lack explicit supporting citations or data.
Some reliance on secondary summaries limits consistently deep interrogation of sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates strong critical analysis (band 3-4) with justified points and use of theory. Occasional unsupported claims prevent the higher band (5-6), warranting a score of 4.

strengths: Conclusion is balanced, consistent with evidence, and acknowledges complexity.
There are clear links between interview insights and theoretical perspectives.
Multiple perspectives (Kurdish, Sunni, Shia) are acknowledged and briefly evaluated.
weaknesses: Synthesis remains at the level of linking rather than integrating into a richer framework.
External perspectives (e.g., regional actors) are noted only superficially.
No concrete policy recommendations or deeper evaluative framework in the conclusion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates solid synthesis and evaluation (band 3-4) by linking experiences and theory and considering multiple viewpoints. Lack of deeper integration and policy insight constrains the score to 3.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The political issue â the extent to which the 2006 Iraqi constitution has exacerbated sectarian and ethnic divisions â is stated explicitly in both the title and the opening paragraph. The focus on âsectarian and ethnic divisionsâ and the temporal marker âsince 2006â leave no ambiguity. 

The student offers a succinct yet precise contextualisation (post-Saddam transition, constitution drafted by interim government and U.S. officials) that shows they clearly recognise what makes this a political issue in global-politics terms (sovereignty, legitimacy). The clarity and specificity fully meet the descriptor for the top band.

The write-up states that two semi-structured interviews were conducted and indicates what each engagement contributed (âpositive perspective on the constitution for Kurdsâ; âimpact on political and social relationshipsâ). This is clearly relevant to the issue, fulfilling the descriptor for band 3-4. The description, however, lacks methodological detail (dates, medium, length), so while clear, it is not fully comprehensive, justifying a score of 3 rather than 4.

Throughout the report the student explicitly references how each interview altered or deepened understanding â e.g., discovering the quota system is âunspoken,â realising Kurdish autonomy nuances, distinguishing political from social tensions. These reflections show a clear, specific explanation of how experiences informed learning, fully matching the top-band descriptor.

The analysis moves beyond description, tracing causal links between constitutional design, quota politics, de-Baathification, SunniâShia dynamics and the rise of ISIS. Key course concepts such as legitimacy, failed state, identity politics and federalism are applied appropriately. While insightful, the exploration occasionally summarises secondary sources rather than interrogating them, so depth is strong but not uniformly sophisticated, warranting 5 (lower end of 5-6 band).

Direct links to course content are explicitly made: the foundational unit (power, sovereignty, legitimacy) and the peace & conflict unit are listed; later, concepts such as legitimacy and failed state are invoked in analysis. Although the mapping is clear, the student does not always weave the theoretical vocabulary deeply into the argument, so the strength of linkage is good but not outstanding, meriting the lower half of the top band.

Most claims are backed by citations (Wilson Center, Carnegie, CIA Factbook) or interview evidence, and counter-arguments (e.g., benefits for Kurds) are weighed. A few assertions (e.g., percentage of Kurds controlling Baghdad) lack explicit support, preventing ânearly allâ points from being justified. This aligns with the top of the 3-4 band.

Kurdish, Sunni and Shia perspectives are acknowledged and the student evaluates the strengths and limitations of each (e.g., considering Kurdish positionality; assessing Sunni grievances). Evaluation is present but sometimes brief, and perspectives outside Iraq (e.g., U.S. policymakers) are noted superficially. Hence solid evidence of evaluation earns the upper band but not 5-6.

The student frequently juxtaposes interview insights with academic literature, demonstrating links between lived experience and theory (e.g., Kurdish activistâs view compared with state-failure literature). This integration is evident but not consistently synthesised into a ârichâ overarching framework; thus it meets, but does not exceed, the 3-4 descriptor.

The student explains personal motivation (Egyptian and Iraqi heritage; desire to understand divisions) and gives brief but relevant reasons for choosing the two engagements: the Kurdish activistâs daughter to obtain a âpositive perspectiveâ and Dr Dagher for a broader analytical view. This satisfies the requirement for a clear explanation, but the justification of why exactly these two voices were most suitable (e.g., their expertise, representativeness, or access) is only moderate, not exhaustiveâhence a solid but not maximum score within the top band.

The conclusion restates the central judgement, recognises complexity (âcatalyst not root causeâ), and remains consistent with evidence presented. Balance is shown by acknowledging benefits for minorities and persistent debate about pre-war unity. Minor repetition and lack of explicit policy recommendations limit sophistication, so a high 3-4 band mark is appropriate.

annotations.comment: The title explicitly and clearly identifies the political issue as the impact of the 2006 constitution on sectarian divisions, demonstrating excellent issue identification.
The inline LaTeX formula appears out of context. Clarify or remove it to avoid confusion in explaining your methodology.
Your reflections in the report section demonstrate a clear explanation of how each engagement informed your understanding, meeting the top band for learning connection.
The table of contents is well structured, but consider adding page numbers or section markers for easier navigation.
The introduction effectively contextualises the issue and connects it to your personal motivation, meeting the topâband criteria for issue identification and justification.
Connections to course content are clear but could be deepened by explicitly weaving theoretical vocabulary into the argument rather than listing units.
The description of the first interview clearly outlines what you did and its relevance to the issue, satisfying the engagement description criterion.
You apply key course concepts such as legitimacy and state failure effectively in analysis, showing strong depth, but avoid overârelying on summary of sources.
A few assertions, such as the illâconsidered drafting timeframe, would benefit from additional citation or quantitative support.
The claim that Kurds have substantial control over Baghdad lacks direct evidence; consider adding a specific source or statistic.
Typographical error âanother anotherâ distracts readers; correct to maintain professionalism.
Footnote formatting varies in style and order of elements; standardize to a consistent citation format.
Incorporating the counterclaim from the Kurdish interview strengthens analysis by acknowledging diverse viewpoints and justifying your argument.
Consider drawing in regional perspectives (e.g., Iranian or Turkish views) to deepen the multiâperspective evaluation.
The final conclusion restates your judgment clearly, but you could enhance impact by offering concrete policy recommendations.
Bibliography entries vary in layout and spacing; unify formatting to improve consistency and readability.
Remove or clarify the stray LaTeX math expression in the course content list to avoid disrupting the narrative.
Footnote 1âs source is partially obscured; please clarify the reference to ensure readers can locate it.
The intermediate conclusion clearly states your emerging argument, demonstrating balanced evaluation and consistency with evidence.
Consider adding a subâheading for the Kirkuk discussion to guide the reader and clarify the shift in focus.
An OCR error in the date â20241â should be corrected to maintain clarity in your footnote.
Your analysis of ISISâs emergence applies theoretical insights well; causal links are clear and appropriately justified.
Link the Kirkuk discussion explicitly to the concept of sovereignty to strengthen the theoretical integration.
Citation styles diverge in the bibliography (e.g., date formats, italics); adopt a single referencing style.
The spelling âIragiâ recurs throughout; standardize to âIraqiâ to maintain accuracy and professional tone.

rubricData.name: Global Politics IA: Engagement Activity
rubricData.description: For the new Global Politics syllabus

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue not identified or irrelevant
2. No meaningful explanation

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: No discussion of why the project matters

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities absent or unrelated to issue

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue named but explanation vague / superficial
2. Limited or missing context

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: Importance / suitability mentioned but weak or generic

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities listed; relevance implied or weakly linked

strands.name: Political issue identification & clarity
strands.content:
1. Issue explicitly stated
2. Clear, precise explanation with relevant context

strands.name: Project importance & suitability
strands.content: Importance / suitability clearly justified with specific reasons

strands.name: Relevance of engagement activities
strands.content: Activities fully explained and convincingly linked to the issue

strands.name: Integration of research â engagement
strands.content: No link between research and engagement

strands.name: Engagement planning
strands.content: Activities planned but timing / logistics patchy

strands.name: Integration of research â engagement
strands.content: Research consistently informs engagement; findings feed back into further inquiry

strands.name: Depth of political-issue analysis
strands.content: Clear, balanced analysis; key factors explored

strands.name: Synthesis of stakeholder perspectives
strands.content: Effective synthesis; perspectives woven into coherent argument

strands.name: Awareness of personal bias
strands.content: Biases explored; impact on work examined

strands.name: Organization & structure
strands.content: Logical structure; mostly easy to follow
